quoteFirst: It was a forward stateroom, and hence not so desirable as those further aft, but still a good room, and the only one that was available. I directed the clerk to have the bourgeois’ baggage put in, and to show him the room when he should request it.
quoteLast: , when the boat was tied up for the night, and I was in the office writing up the journal, the bourgeois came in and asked the clerk for his room. The clerk took him out and showed him his room and told him that two of the clerks had given it up for him.
title:
History of Early Steamboat Navigation on the Missouri River, Volume 1 (of 2)
Life and Adventures of Joseph La Barge
author: Chittenden, Hiram Martin, 1858-1917

quoteFirst: “I see,” he said, “there is an afternoon train which arrives at Disley
quoteTime: about nine o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: . If that would suit you equally well, I should prefer it.”
title: Phemie Keller
author: Riddell, J. H., Mrs., 1832-1906

quoteFirst: April 9th.—About 9 o’clock we were off again, halting at 5.30 p.m. at Adatur, on the River Bogoo. A fine leopard and hyæna were shot last night.
quoteLast: , whilst I was writing my diary, alone in camp, the others having gone out in search of lions, a native came up to me, whispering, “Asset, asset, howaga” (a lion, a lion, sir). It was a lovely moonlight night, so light that I could see to read or write distinctly.
title:
Life in the Soudan
Adventures Amongst the Tribes, and Travels in Egypt, in 1881 and 1882
author: Williams, Josiah

quoteFirst: As it turned out, there was no opportunity for any of these amiable schemes to be carried into effect, for the very next day Honor was taken suddenly worse
quoteTime: about nine o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: , and to all who saw her it seemed clear that the end had at last really come. There was great dismay amongst those who were drawn to the cabin by the news, not so much on account of the fact itself, as on account of the difficulty, the perennial difficulty at Inishmaan, of getting a priest across from the larger island in time.
title: Grania, The Story of an Island; vol. 2/2
author: Lawless, Emily, 1845-1913

quoteTime: About nine o'clock in the evening

title: Lord Montagu's Page: An Historical Romance

quoteTime: about nine o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: , the flame made by a number of bunches of straw, which the marquis had fired, made the courtyard as light as day. title: The Milkmaid of Montfermeil (Novels of Paul de Kock Volume XX)

quoteTime: About nine o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: , a most dismal fire broke out in a barn in which a great number of persons were met together to see a puppet-show. In the barn there were a great many loads of new light straw; the barn was thatched with straw, which was very dry, and the inner roof of the barn was covered with old dry cobwebs; so that the fire, like lightning, flew round the barn in an instant, and there was but one small door belonging to the barn, which was close nailed up, and could not be easily broke open; and when it was opened, the passage was so narrow, and every body so impatient to escape, that the door was
